claim: Rust generics are compiled by monomorphization into concrete type-specific versions for used types, giving runtime behavior like concrete code but with possible compile-time/code-size tradeoffs not covered by the basic source.
positive_scope: Evidence for static specialization as an optimizer-friendly generic mechanism.
negative_scope: Does not quantify code bloat, compile time, or cross-crate optimization limits.

claim: Rust trait objects carry a data pointer plus method table/vtable and calls through dyn Trait use runtime virtual dispatch, which the Rust Book says adds lookup cost and prevents inlining of the method body, blocking some optimizations.
positive_scope: Evidence that dynamic dispatch has optimizer-visible representation/cost and should be explicit when used.
negative_scope: Trait objects provide heterogeneity and compile-time trait checking; exact ABI/devirtualization rules are not specified by these pages.

claim: Rust restricts which traits and methods can be used for trait-object dispatch: dyn-compatible traits exclude Self:Sized requirements, associated constants, generic dispatchable methods, opaque returns, and unsupported receivers.
positive_scope: Evidence that a language can restrict dynamic dispatch interfaces to make runtime dispatch representation coherent.
negative_scope: These restrictions are semantic/type-system rules, not direct proof of optimization benefit.

claim: Rust codegen units, incremental compilation, and LTO expose a compile-time/runtime tradeoff: more separate units can speed compilation but may inhibit optimization, while LTO enables LLVM whole-program analysis at longer link time.
positive_scope: Evidence that specialization/inlining optimization depends not only on language semantics but compilation-unit and LTO boundaries.
negative_scope: Does not identify specific lost optimizations for generics; compiler options are implementation-specific.

claim: .NET generics are reified enough that CIL contains generic metadata and reflection can inspect type arguments; the runtime specializes value-type instantiations but shares a reference-type generic body using object references.
positive_scope: Evidence for a hybrid generic implementation: specialization for value types, sharing for reference types, runtime metadata preserved.
negative_scope: Does not prove specific JIT optimizations; reflection metadata may constrain closed-world assumptions unless restricted.

claim: C# sealed classes prevent inheritance and sealed overrides prevent further overriding, giving semantic finality information; the fetched Microsoft source does not itself claim devirtualization benefits.
positive_scope: Evidence for source-level finality constraints relevant to possible dispatch reasoning.
negative_scope: Do not overclaim optimizer benefit without separate JIT/compiler evidence.

claim: GCC documents devirtualization from virtual calls to direct calls, speculative guarded devirtualization, indirect inlining after targets become known, and LTO/IPA mechanisms that increase cross-file visibility for inlining and propagation.
positive_scope: Evidence that backend compilers can consume finality/type-profile/whole-program facts to reduce dynamic dispatch overhead.
negative_scope: Compiler- and option-dependent; speculative devirtualization may be reverted, and LTO/IPA costs compile time/memory/code size.

claim: GCC documents that ELF semantic interposition can restrict interprocedural analysis, inlining, and propagation for exported symbols, while -fno-semantic-interposition assumes interposed definitions preserve semantics.
positive_scope: Evidence that ABI/dynamic-linking semantics can block optimizer assumptions even when source code appears statically known.
negative_scope: ELF/GCC-specific; source-language design must separately choose module/ABI/interposition rules.

claim: Julia performance guidance emphasizes type-stable functions, concrete fields/containers, function barriers, and careful specialization because abstract or changing types and Function-typed fields hide representation/call targets and can cause dynamic dispatch or indirect storage.
positive_scope: Evidence for a dynamic/multiple-dispatch language recovering performance through type inference and specialization when types are stable and concrete.
negative_scope: Heuristic and workload-dependent; over-specialization can increase compile-time/cache pressure.

claim: Julia dispatch selects methods by all positional argument types, and broad methods may still be compiled separately for concrete argument tuples so downstream calls can be resolved ahead of execution; world age and unlimited cases constrain this model.
positive_scope: Evidence that dynamic multiple dispatch can coexist with compiler specialization.
negative_scope: Methods listed by users are not the same as compiler specializations; method redefinition/world age and unlimited cases complicate optimizer assumptions.
